topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop hummed with the usual lunchtime chatter, but my focus was entirely on Liam. He was laughing, a bright, open sound that used to be the soundtrack to my entire adolescence. I watched him across the small table, tracing the rim of my mug with a restless finger. When he finally turned to me, his smile was genuine, warm, but something in his eyes felt… distant. I offered a casual greeting, the words tasting dry on my tongue. "Hey, Liam. How's everything?" He blinked, a brief flicker of confusion crossing his face before he defaulted to a generic answer about work. A cold knot tightened in my chest. I leaned in, trying to coax a memory out, a shared inside joke, anything. He offered a polite shrug, a slight hesitation in his voice that felt like a deliberate wall going up. I pulled back, the warmth draining from my expression, and started scanning the room, looking for an explanation I couldn't quite name.

[story 2]

I found myself hovering near the park bench where we used to launch bottle rockets. Liam was there, sketching in a notebook, the familiar way he held his charcoal stick oddly stiff. I approached slowly, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the gravel path. "Remember that time we tried to build that ridiculous fort?" I asked, my voice carefully level, trying to keep the tremor out of it. He looked up, his gaze meeting mine for a beat too long. A faint, almost imperceptible tightening around his jaw made my stomach clench. He shook his head, a small, dismissive gesture. "Forts? I don't think so. What are you talking about?" The air between us seemed to thicken, heavy and awkward. I noticed the way his eyes darted away just as I tried to press further, a subtle avoidance that spoke volumes. I took a breath, forcing a lightness into my tone, but the effort felt exhausting.

[story 3]

It was the reunion party, a forced gathering orchestrated by mutual acquaintances. I spotted Liam near the punch bowl, looking effortlessly charming, the way he always did when he was trying to charm a room. I walked over, my practiced smile feeling brittle. "Liam! Wow, it's been ages." I watched him nod, his expression pleasant, but utterly devoid of the spark I remembered. I tried to recount a ridiculous anecdote from high school, something that should have instantly bridged the gap. But the words caught in my throat. He listened patiently, nodding along, but his eyes remained fixed on some point just beyond my shoulder, as if I were a stranger reciting trivia. A profound sense of dislocation washed over me. I mumbled a quick goodbye, turning away before I could ask another question, the silence of my retreat louder than any conversation could have been.
